editableNewDialogue.text: Can you quiz me quickly on AKI causes, but keep it practical?
Sure—I'll do short clinical vignettes. For each one, tell me whether it’s prerenal, intrinsic, or postrenal, and name the likely cause. Ready?
Case 1: After several days of vomiting and diarrhea, a patient becomes dizzy with low BP and reduced urine output; creatinine rises. What type of AKI and the cause?
Yes. Case 2: Another patient has AKI after receiving gentamicin; urinalysis shows granular “muddy brown” casts. Type and cause?
Correct. Case 3: A patient has flank pain, ultrasound shows bilateral hydronephrosis, and urine output is low. Type and cause?
Postrenal obstruction, maybe stone or prostate.

Exactly—postrenal due to obstruction (stone/tumor/clot in ureters, or bladder outlet obstruction like BPH). If you want, I can continue with 7 more cases.

Unrelated examples (not about you): (1) In a 2012 London case report, ethylene glycol ingestion caused severe intrinsic AKI via oxalate crystal deposition. (2) A fictional ICU scenario often used in teaching has septic shock causing prerenal AKI that progresses to ATN if prolonged. (3) Some hospitals use a “renal traffic light” chart in wards to flag nephrotoxins and prompt dose adjustments.
make them as short OSCE stems please—i’m trying to fit them onto my flashcards.
Got it—I'll write 15 short OSCE-style AKI etiology Q&As with brief stems and one-line answers.
btw quick correction—i’m not a GP doctor. i’m still a 5th-year medical student; i just said GP before to keep it simple.
Thanks for clarifying. So you’re a 5th-year medical student (not a GP). I’ll stick to student-level clinical scenarios and explanations.
